coredata.dc:title: High fluorescence quantum yield of methionine-doped carbon quantum dots for achieving rapid assay of tetracyclines in foodstuffs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yawei
Xie, Xinying
Wang, Xiaofei
Wang, Chaoyi
Gao, Ming

Qin, Hongbing
Wang, Xuedong
Wang, Junxia
coredata.dc:description: The trace-level detection of tetracyclines (TCs) in food products is essential to ensure food safety and public health. Herein, we prepared the methionine-doped carbon quantum dots (Met-CQDs) using citric acid as the precursor. Met-CQDs exhibited a Gaussian unimodal peak centered at 440 nm in the fluorescent excitation spectrum, along with a remarkable greenish-blue emission and a fluorescent quantum yield of 33.5 %. Furthermore, the presence of TC (the quencher) caused a rapid quenching of the fluorescence of Met-CQDs, accompanying with a color transition from light blue to dark bule as TC concentrations increased. The coloring variation was also detected by the images captured by smartphones and RGB analysis software, facilitating portable detection of TC utilizing Met-CQDs as a fluoroprobe. The findings indicate that the Met-CQDs based fluoroprobe exhibits high selectivity, rapid response (only ∼1 min) according to an “ON-OFF” sensing model. This fluorescence sensing method gave a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.032 μM and excellent linearity for TC in the concentration range of 0.1–500 μM. Also, the smartphone-based fluorescence-visualizing approach displayed good linearity with a LOD of 0.33 μM. The interactions between this fluoroprobe and TC occurred by virtue of both inner filter effect (IFE) and static-quenching principle. The average recovery for TC in the milk, honey, and tap water samples was determined to be 98.46 ± 1.71 % by a fluorometric method. Overall, both fluorometric and RGB approaches demonstrate strong correlation with conventional LC-MS/MS, and thus the as-fabricated Met-CQDs are promising for the preliminary screening of TCs’ residues in food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Amino acid
High quantum yield
Inner filter effect
Intelligent visualization
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Met-CQDs for TC sensing based on “ON-OFF” model
Linearity of TC using fluorometric method and smartphone visualization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research received collaborative support from several entities, including the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22076134 and 32202148 ), the Jiangsu Provincial Key Research & Development Program ( BE2022733 ), the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( BK20211338 ), as well as the Key Science and Technology Project of Suzhou City ( SS202028 ). Additionally, support came from Suzhou National Joint Laboratory focused on Green and Low-carbon Wastewater Treatment and Resource Utilization Technology ( SZLSDT202404 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of hydrothermal synthesis of Met-CQDs (a) Different molar ratios of CA and Met, (b) reaction time, (c) reaction temperature.

Morphology and structural characterization of Met-CQDs (a) TEM images (illustration: high-resolution TEM images and Particle size distribution); (b) XRD pattern; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d) Total spectrum of XPS; (e) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s; (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s; (g) High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s; (h) High-resolution XPS spectra of S2p.

Optical property of Met-CQDs (a) Fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption (UV–vis) spectra; (b) Fluorescence spectra of Met-CQDs as the excitation wavelength from 310 to 350 nm; (c) three-dimensional contour fluorescence spectra; (d) Diagram of corresponding luminescence chroma coordinate.
Photoluminescence Stability of Met-CQDs. (a) Different concentration NaCl; (b) Different pH; (c) Storage time; (d) Duration time exposure to UV radiation.

Selectivity and fluorescence detection linearity of Met-CQDs for TC detection (a) Selectivity of fluorescent probes for the detection of TC (Concentrations of TC, amino acids and other ions were 600 μM); (b) Specificity of Met-CQDs fluorescent probes for TC detection in the presence of coexisting interfering substances (their concentrations of 1000 μM); (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of Met-CQDs after adding TC concentration (0–800 μM); (d) The regression curve between (F0 − F1)/F0 values and TC concentration.
Linear relationship between gray value using smartphone sensor platform and TC concentration from 1 to 600 μM.

Fluorescence quenching mechanisms of Met-CQDs (a) The ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–Vis) spectra of TC and the excitation and emission spectra of Met-CQDs; (b) The ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–Vis) spectra of Met-CQDs and Met-CQDs + TC, respectively; (c) Stern–Volmer equation curve; (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves before and after the addition of TC (200 μM); (e) the Zeta potential without and with TC added.
Preparation of methionine-doped carbon quantum dots and their application for fluorescence detection of tetracycline.
The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) of TC in milk, honey and tap water (n = 3) by fluorometric method.
Comparison of the detected using both fluorometer and smartphone with those by LC-MS/MS.

row.entry: Detected by fluorometry based on Met-CQDs (μM)
Detected by RGB approach based on Met-CQDs (μM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: Relative accuracy denotes the relative percentages of the values detected by fluoroprobe based on Met-CQDs through both a fluorometer and smartphone to those by LC-MS/MS.

head.ce:title: High fluorescence quantum yield of methionine-doped carbon quantum dots for achieving rapid assay of tetracyclines in foodstuffs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou National Joint Laboratory for Green and Low-carbon Wastewater Treatment and Resource Utilization Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou National Joint Laboratory for Green and Low-carbon Wastewater Treatment and Resource Utilization Technology
School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou National Joint Laboratory for Green and Low-carbon Wastewater Treatment and Resource Utilization Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Suzhou University of Science and Technology, No. 99, Xuefu Road, Suzhou 215009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 Preparation of methionine-doped carbon quantum dots and their application for fluorescence detection of tetracycline.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The trace-level detection of tetracyclines (TCs) in food products is essential to ensure food safety and public health. Herein, we prepared the methionine-doped carbon quantum dots (Met-CQDs) using citric acid as the precursor. Met-CQDs exhibited a Gaussian unimodal peak centered at 440 nm in the fluorescent excitation spectrum, along with a remarkable greenish-blue emission and a fluorescent quantum yield of 33.5 %. Furthermore, the presence of TC (the quencher) caused a rapid quenching of the fluorescence of Met-CQDs, accompanying with a color transition from light blue to dark bule as TC concentrations increased. The coloring variation was also detected by the images captured by smartphones and RGB analysis software, facilitating portable detection of TC utilizing Met-CQDs as a fluoroprobe. The findings indicate that the Met-CQDs based fluoroprobe exhibits high selectivity, rapid response (only ∼1 min) according to an “ON-OFF” sensing model. This fluorescence sensing method gave a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.032 μM and excellent linearity for TC in the concentration range of 0.1–500 μM. Also, the smartphone-based fluorescence-visualizing approach displayed good linearity with a LOD of 0.33 μM. The interactions between this fluoroprobe and TC occurred by virtue of both inner filter effect (IFE) and static-quenching principle. The average recovery for TC in the milk, honey, and tap water samples was determined to be 98.46 ± 1.71 % by a fluorometric method. Overall, both fluorometric and RGB approaches demonstrate strong correlation with conventional LC-MS/MS, and thus the as-fabricated Met-CQDs are promising for the preliminary screening of TCs’ residues in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are broad-spectrum antibiotics that primarily inhibit bacterial protein synthesis, and they are widely employed in livestock, poultry, aquatic organisms, and other industries due to their potential bacteriostatic effect and cost-effectiveness [1]. However, an excessive and inappropriate use of TCs biocides triggers their residual accumulation in various food products, which are further concentrated in human body via food chain. Some species of TCs are capable of binding with calcium and have adverse effects on human health [2–5]. To ensure food safety, the maximum residual limits (MRLs) for TCs’ residues in animal-derived foods have been regulated in many countries. For instance, the EU and China have set the MRLs for TCs in milk at 0.225 µM [6], while the USA has set it at 0.676 µM [7]. The analytical methods for TCs’ residues in honey and milk have been widely reported, such as liquid chromatography (LC) [8,9], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10], and fluorescence polarization immunoassay [11]. LC methods usually require specialized and sophisticated equipment and time-consuming pre-treatment procedures. Although immunoassays can offer high specificity and sensitivity as well as low detection limit, limited selectivity and high synthesis costs have limited their wide applications. Therefore, the development of a rapid, reliable, economical, and portable approach for TCs detection in foods is essential for their safety evaluation.

Recently, there has been an increasing focus on fluorescence assays due to their exceptional sensitivity, rapid and straightforward operation, as well as convenient on-site detection [12]. The fluorescence assays have been extensively reported for the detection of TCs, including metal nanoclusters [13], semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdTe) [14], metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [15,16], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [17,18]. However, secondary contamination due to metal leaching limits the widespread use of metal-based fluorescent materials. CQDs are highly favored for their exceptional biocompatibility, good water solubility, low toxicity, and highly tunable optical properties for the sensitive detection of TCs. For example, Venugopalan et al. [18] utilized a microwave-assisted method to synthesize wild lemon-derived CQDs for TC detection, and the minimum detectable limit of TC in water samples was determined to be 0.42 µM. However, the limited fluorescent quantum yield and the lack of surface functional groups in CQDs derived from natural materials have limited their applications in the analysis field [19].

Heteroatom doping can effectively enhance the fluorescence properties [20–22], and especially the N-doped CQDs have earned much plentiful eyes [23,24]. Furthermore, it is widely accepted that the N-doping leads to structural defective formation and additional energy levels, thereby augmenting the amount of electrons in the conduction band [25]. As an illustration, a fluoroprobe-based N-CQDs was employed to detect TC via inner filter effect (IFE), accompanied with a detection limit of 0.344 µM, but a low quantum yield of 10.97 % [24]. Although N-doping can improve the fluorescent performance of CQDs, the nitrogen content and formation of CN bonds are the key factors affecting quantum yield [26]. A co-doping strategy is often employed to further enhance the specific properties of CQDs [27]. S-doping can enhance both the stability and nitrogen content of CQDs [28]. Co-doping with N and S has been demonstrated to be one of the most promising and attractive strategies [24,27–30]. In this context, Zhao and colleagues [30] fabricated a fluoroprobe of N, S-codoped CQDs using glutathione as a dopant to detect TC in milk, yielding a wide linear range (0.1–65 µM) and a low detection limit (0.04 µM). Among various precursors, amino acids (AAs) are considered to be feasible for producing N-CQDs with excellent optical properties due to their renewable, abundant, cost-effective, and non-hazardous characteristics [31]. N, S-codoped CQDs are often prepared with common AAs, such as L-cysteine (Cys) [32] or methionine (Met) [33] as dopants, to produce high quantum yield. In contrast, Met is a S-containing amino acid, and thus N and S are simultaneously incorporated into the carbon structure, without the need of additional chemicals. Methionine-doped CQDs (Met-CQDs) have been effectively used for various metal sensing; however, their applications in analyzing emerging pollutants (such as antibiotics and pesticides) remain limited. Therefore, there is still significant demand for develop fluoroprobe based on Met-CQDs with high sensitivity and selectivity for tetracycline (TC) detection.

Herein, a flexible and rapid fluoroprobe with high quantum yield was fabricated using a low-cost strategy for TCs’ residual detection in food products. In addition, the crucial factors such as the molar ratio of CA and Met along with the reaction temperature and duration were meticulously optimized in order to enhance their fluorescence efficiency. The as-constructed CQDs exhibit a striking yellow-green when exposed to sunlight, a blue fluorescence under a UV lamp with a distinct quantum yield of 33.52 %. After adding TCs (serving as the quencher) into the fluoroprobe system, the fluorescence of Met-CQDs was rapidly quenched. This property served as a valuable tool for the identification and quantification of TCs, since the decreasing fluorescence intensity was closely correlated to the concentration of quenchers. A highly selective and sensitive fluoroprobe based Met-CQDs was developed for sensing TC according to the “ON-OFF” principle. Furthermore, the fluoroprobe was applied for the portable detection of TC in food through a smartphone-based colorimetric sensing platform (RGB method) (Scheme 1 ). Meanwhile, the concentrations of TC in the milk, honey and tap-water samples were determined by fluorometric and RGB colorimetric methods, and the results were compared with those of the conventional LC-MS/MS method. The successful application of the proposed fluoroprobe provides a novel potential for rapid screening of antibiotic residues in foodstuff.

The standard solutions of three TCs including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC), were acquired from Shanghai Titan Technology (Adamas, Shanghai, China). CA and AAs, such as Cys, serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His) and Met, were procured from Titan Technology (Shanghai, China). The following chemical, ions and solvent were sourced from Aladdin Biochemical Agents (Shanghai, China): including spectral-grade potassium bromide (KBr), various ions (Al3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, and MnO4 −), and chromatographic-grade acetonitrile.

The Met-CQDs solution was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using Met and CA as precursors [33]. Briefly, 0.12 g CA and 0.037 g Met were accurately weighed and homogenously mixed with 30 mL of ultrapure water under a 10-min ultrasonication. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to a 6-h reaction at 200 °C. After that, it was cooled down and collected in a centrifuge tube. To remove impurities, the final reaction mixture was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 10 min. After centrifugation, the solution was filtered through a 0.45-μm needle membrane to further purify the substance. The mixture was then dialyzed for 24 h in the dark using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da. As the solution became dark during dialysis, ultrapure water was added and dialysis continued until the solution became clear. The purified solution was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator and sheltered from outer light.

The Met-CQDs solution was dried by the freeze-drying apparatus (FD-1A-50, Biocool, Beijing). The lattice fringes and particle size of the Met-CQDs were recorded by transmission electron microscopy (FEI Talos F200X G2, USA). An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) was used to analyze elemental composition, content, valence state, and molecular structure. Surface groups were examined by mixing Met-CQDs with KBr powder in a ratio of 1:200, followed by grinding and analysis using a FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS5, NY, USA) with KBr as the control. The fluorescent and UV–vis spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS-5, Edinburgh, England) and a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-5500PC, Yuanxiao, China). Fluorescence lifetimes of the Met-CQDs solution and its mixed solution with TC at excitation wavelength of 420 nm were recorded using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, England).

To achieve the optimized fluorescence spectra, we investigated the fluorescence emission spectra of Met-CQDs under excitation wavelengths between 310 and 350 nm and with a 3-cm slit width. The fluorescence intensities (FIs) of Met-CQDs were recorded under various aqueous solutions including pH levels (2–12), salt concentrations (0–1 M), and different storage times (15, 30, 45 and 60 d) at 4 °C, as well as under UV irradiation to ensure their emission stability. The quantum yield of as-constructed Met-CQDs was determined by employing quinine sulfate as a reference standard with a known yield of 54 % [34]. Detailed methods are provided in the TEXT 1 in Supplementary Information (SI).

Fluorescence assay was conducted by preparing a fluoroprobe system consisting of 100 μL of Met-CQDs in 2.2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 4.0). Hereafter, 100 μL of TC aqueous solution was added to the established fluoroprobe system. This mixture was incubated at 20 °C for 2 min before being placed in a fluorescent colorimetric dish for analysis. The emission of Met-CQDs was measured under excitation at 340 nm. Their FIs were recorded at an emission wavelength of 420 nm. In parallel, we used 100 μL of ultrapure water without TC as a control group to effectively compare the variations in the FIs resulting from the presence of tetracycline.

Met-CQDs solution was poured into a 96-well plate and combined with TC solution at varying concentrations. The plate was then exposed to 365-nm UV radiation. Images of each well plate were captured using a smartphone for scanning recognition and analyzed using color recognition software to convert them into digital information, such as RGB values and HSL values. Finally, a standard curve was constructed by plotting TC concentrations (0–800 μM) vs. quenched rates (F0 − F1)/F0 or the RGB values.

Milk and honey were purchased from a grocery store in Suzhou City, China, and pretreated according to China standard guidelines (GB 31658.6-2021 [35] and GB/T 18932.23-2003 [36]. Specifically, 5 mL of acetonitrile was incorporated into 2.5 mL of milk, mixed for 30 s and then centrifuged at 4,000 rpm for 5 min. The resulting mixture was passed through a 0.22-μm needle filtration and diluted 10-fold with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 4.0) to reduce the pigment concentration in the milk. For the honey extraction, 1 g of honey sample was mixed with 5 mL of Na2EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer (pH = 4.0) and centrifuged for 10 min. Aliquots (5 mL) of the resulting solution were collected. Additionally, a tap water sample (5 mL) was obtained from an environmental laboratory and simply filtered. Finally, a portion of 200 μL from the diluted milk and honey solution was added to the fluoroprobe solution.

The recovery for TC in the spiked samples was investigated to assess accuracy of the proposed method. For this purpose, the milk, honey, and water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of TC. These samples were pretreated and analyzed according to the previously described procedure. The TC content of each sample was meticulously determined three times using the fluorometric, RGB and LC-MS/MS techniques, thus ensuring a comprehensive assessment of the method's effectiveness across different sample types.

As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the FIs were significantly enhanced after the introduction methionine into CQDs. Furthermore, the concentration of methionine (precursors of N-doping) also exerted a direct influence on the emission intensity of Met-CQDs. The maximum emission intensity was observed when the ratio of CA to Met was 1:3 (Fig. 1 a). However, a slight decline in the FIs was observed with further increase in the ratio. To further unravel the intrinsic reason for the enhancement of CQDs’ FIs in the presence of methionine, we employed the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy to calculate the band gap energies of Met-CQDs and CQDs according to the transversal method, as detailed in TEXT 2 in the SI. It was clear that the decrease of band gap energy from 5.34 to 5.25 eV after introducing methionine into the CQDs (Fig. S1b and c). This reduction could be attributed to the formation of impurity energy levels in the band gap, which were beneficial for the enhanced fluorescence of CQDs and connected to the conduction band as a result of doping [37,38]. Under the molar ratio of 1:3, the FIs of the Met-CQDs reached a peak value of ∼1.3 × 105 a.u. after a hydrothermal reaction time of 6 h at 350-nm excitation (Fig. 1b). Prolongation of the reaction time beyond 6 h may alter their surface chemical states, resulting in a decrease in the intensities of the Met-CQDs. The components were heated at temperatures between 140 °C and 220 °C. As depicted in Fig. 1c, reactions that were too low (<160 °C) produced fewer luminescent particles. The optimum FI for the Met-CQDs was recorded at a temperature of about 180 °C. Increasing temperature facilitated deeper carbonization and the development of a crystalline structure for Met-CQDs. The FI and quantum yield can be improved to some extent by adjustment of the reaction time and temperature based on their surface chemical state.

The Met-CQDs exhibited a well-dispersed spherical morphology as indicated by HRTEM analysis, with particle sizes ranging from 1.2 to 3.8 nm and an average of 2.713 ± 0.203 nm. They exhibited a Gaussian distribution reflecting a normal size distribution among the particles (Fig. 2 a). The lattice planes displayed a spacing of 0.1823 nm, which corresponds to the typical lattice spacing of the (100) plane of sp2 graphite materials [39]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the Met-CQDs peaked at 2θ = 21.742° (Fig. 2b), suggesting that these materials have a structure characteristic of typical amorphous carbon [40].

As for the FT-IR spectra of Met-CQDs (Fig. 2c), two distinct broad peaks at 3467 and 3421 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups [41]. Additionally, a prominent peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of NH, while the peak at 1720 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibrations of the carbonyl group. Furthermore, the peaks at 1405 and 1073 cm−1 were indicative of the stretching vibrations associated with CN and CO bonds, respectively [42]. These peaks indicated the presence of amide groups (CONH), suggesting the incorporation of amino acids during the preparation of Met-CQDs [43]. Furthermore, two additional peaks located at 1270 and 2320 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of CSC and SH functional groups, respectively [29,40,44]. Overall, the distinct infrared spectra of the Met-CQDs revealed that the synthesized materials were composed of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amide, and mercaptan, which enhanced the solubility in water and supported practical applications.

XPS spectra revealed the chemical composition and valence state of Met-CQDs (Fig. 2d). Four distinct signals were identified with binding energies of 400.8 eV, 399.9 eV, 284.8 eV, and 163.8 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p elements, respectively. It was worth noting that methionine, with a relative content of 9.94 % and 9.01 % for N and S, substantially increased S-doping content. The C1s spectrum accounted for the highest percentage of 57.1 % (Fig. 2e), in which four peaks were observed, including 284.2 eV for CC or CC, 285.8 eV for CN or CO, 287.8 eV for CO, and 284.9 eV for C-S [44,45]. The Ols spectrum represented 23.99 % of total atoms (Fig. 2g), of which two peaks appeared at 529.8 eV for CO or COH and at 533.3 eV for OCO. Furthermore, the N1s spectrum presented three distinct peaks (Fig. 2f): 399.1 eV for NH2, 400.1 eV for CNC, and 401.7 eV for graphite nitrogen [44]. The S2p spectrum demonstrated two peaks at 163.7 eV for S2pl/2 and 164.6 eV for S3p3/2 (Fig. 2h) [44,45]. These data confirmed that the Met-CQDs were successfully synthesized.

The UV–vis absorption of Met-CQDs exhibited a sharp high-energy absorption peak at 236 nm and a broad low-energy absorption peak around 323 nm due to the electronic transitions n–σ* and n–π* (Fig. 3 a). Specifically, the peak at 236 nm was related to NH or SH [46], while the 323-nm peak was attributable to electronic transition from n to π* of the sp2 hybridized carbon-heteroatom bond (CO and CN) [43]. The Met-CQDs in the final reaction mixture exhibited a slightly yellow color under white light, transforming to a strong green–blue fluorescence under a 365-nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a).

In Fig. 3b, the Met-CQDs exhibited a highly intense emission spectrum at ∼420 nm under the excitation of 310–350 nm, while emission wavelength shifted to 440 nm, accompanied by a decrease in their FIs when excited at 340–350 nm. The as-fabricated Met-CQDs possessed the excitation-dependent fluorescence in the range of 420–440 nm. The molecular fluorophores produced excitation-dependent emission that remained on the surface of CQDs at both the core and the defect sites.

The fluorescence behavior of Met-CQDs was influenced by their surface structure. For example, amino acid doping and defective sites located on the surface could capture the excited state energy, leading to Met-CQDs’ quantum yield up to 33.5 % (Fig. S2) Fig. 3b also show wide excitonic emission peaks centered at 420 nm with extremely broad full width at half maximum (FWHM) values of ∼120 nm. However, the detailed mechanisms of the broadband fluorescence spectra remained unclear. This phenomenon was probably due to the complex nonradiative excited-state relaxation process arising from specific structure-associated luminescence. To determine whether the broad emission band of Met-CQDs resulted from the relaxation of same excited state, the time-resolved fluorescence-decay spectrum was recorded using a time-correlated single-photon counting technique at different emission wavelengths of 310, 340, 420, and 600 nm for Met-CQDs (Fig. S3). The average value of τem at a given λem was determined via integration of the fluorescence-decay curve using equations (TEXT4 in SI). The τem values at the emission wavelengths of 340 nm were prominently larger than those at 310, 420 and 600 nm (Table S1), indicating that the amount of emitted photons at λem < 420 nm is greater than that at λem = 420 nm. Met-CQDs exhibited the highest τem of 2.36 ns at λem = 340 nm, while the smallest τem of 1.09 ns at λem = 420 nm, presenting no significant difference. These findings indicated that the broad emission peak of Met-CQDs probably resulted from the relaxation of same excited state [47], where transient defects formed in the excited states stabilized the photogenerated charge carriers [48]. Furthermore, the 3D fluorescence spectra further confirmed that the 420-nm FIs occurred at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 3c). Additionally, the intense blue fluorescence of Met-CQDs was illustrated by colorimetric coordinates (0.2163, 0.2124; Fig. 3d).

Obviously, the FIs of the as-constructed Met-CQDs changed slightly with increasing salt concentrations from 0 to 1 M (Fig. 4 a), indicating the remarkable stability of Met-CQDs in salt solution and their reliability as a platform for target detection in high-salt food. As shown in Fig. 4b, the optimized FI was found to be at pH = 4. Both highly acidic and alkaline conditions (pH < 4 and pH > 10) were detrimental to the stability of Met-CQDs by altering their surface charge. Fig. 4c illustrates that the Met-CQDs stored at 4 °C for 60 d, exhibited negligible changes in their FIs, indicating their remarkable long-term stability. Upon exposure to UV irradiation, their FIs decreased by no more than 5 % as shown in Fig. 4d, highlighting their exceptional fluorescence stability. The quantum yield of Met-CQDs was 33.52 %, which was significantly higher than that of other N-CQDs [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Met-CQDs for TC sensing based on “ON-OFF” model

ce:section.ce:para: To improve the sensitivity of the fluorometric assay, the experimental parameters such as solution pH, Met-CQDs volume, incubation temperature, and time, were optimized using the one-factor-at-a-time approach. The degree of quenching in the fluoroprobe system was evaluated using the formula (F0-F1)/F0, where F0 and FI represent the intensities of the Met-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC, respectively.

After addition of 100 μL Met-CQDs and incubation at 20 °C for 1 min, the (F0-F1)/F0 values were evaluated over the pH range of 3.0–10.0 using Tris-HCl buffer (Fig. S4a). Distinctly, the significant fluorescence quenching was observed at pH 6.0. In addition, the (F0 − F1)/F0 values varied with incubation temperatures, initially rise and then reduction as the temperatures increased from 5 to 35 °C (Fig. S4b). Importantly, the maximum (F0 − F1)/F0 value of 0.28 occurred at 20 °C, indicating that it was the appropriate incubation temperature for the fluoroprobe system. Furthermore, Fig. S4c displays that (F0 − F1)/F0 peaked at 200 μL of Met-CQDs, facilitating the subsequent TC assay. It was also noted that the values of (F0 − F1)/F0 varied considerably during the first minute of incubation and then stabilized. Therefore, an incubation period of 1 min was found to be optimal for the accurate detection of TC (Fig. S4d). In summary, the optimal conditions for the detection of TC were a pH 6.0 with Tris-HCl buffer, 200 μL Met-CQDs, and 1-min incubation at 20 °C.

Natural TCs (TC, CTC, and OTC) possess the similarly molecular structures, such as hydrogenated tetracyclic rings, a keto group, an enol group, a phenolic hydroxyl group, a carboxyamide moiety, and other functional groups (Fig. S5). To evaluate the selectivity for sensing TCs antibiotics (TC, CTC, OTC), 100 μL of small molecules (Cys, Ser, Thr, Trp, His), and various ions (Al3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, MnO4 −) were each added to the fluoroprobe system to compare their FIs. Under identical experimental conditions, the emission intensities of Met-CQDs were not obviously decreased when 500 μM of the small molecules and various ions were added into the fluoroprobe system, except for TCs antibiotics (Fig. 5 a), suggesting that the fluoroprobe-based Met-CQDs demonstrated the specific selectivity for sensing TCs antibiotics.

Simultaneously, the anti-interference capability of as-constructed fluoroprobe for detecting TC was evaluated by introducing various co-existing interferents at each individual concentration of 1000 µmol L−1 (e.g., above small molecules and various ions) into the fluoroprobe system. The FIs of Met-CQDs in the co-existing interferents were then compared with and without the addition of TC (500 µmol L−1). Their FIs remained stable in the various interferants, but decreased rapidly after adding TC (Fig. 5b). These observations showed that high concentrations of interfering substances did not significantly affect the quenching effect of Met-CQDs, which further confirmed their high suitability for TC sensing in complex matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity of TC using fluorometric method and smartphone visualization
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 5c, the fluorescence quenching of Met-CQDs was observed after spiking with various concentrations (0–800 µM) of TC. Under optimal conditions, a good linear equation ((F0 − F1)/F0 = 0.00107[TC] + 0.01294, R2 = 0.9930) was observed between the TC concentration (0.1–500 µM) and the (F0-F1)/F0 value (Fig. 5d). The LOD of TC was calculated as 3δ/S according to IUPAC in TEXT 3 in the SI, which is a low LOD of 0.032 µM.

A portable optical sensing platform using UV lamp and color software (RGB method) on smartphones was developed to meet the requirements of on-site and real-time assay without the need for cumbersome instruments. Varying concentrations of TC were introduced into a porous plate containing fluoroprobe buffer solution. After exposure to UV radiation, images of different colors were captured using a smartphone and processed using color extraction software (Image J). To avoid the disturbing effect of external light, we designed the dark box for images acquisition (Fig. S6). The grey values were calculated according to the equation (Gray = 0.30R + 0.59 G + 0.11B) [50], and then utilized to generate a standard curve. A remarkable linearity existed over the concentration range from 1 to 600 µM (Grey = −0.1509 [TC] + 181.61, R2 = 0.995) with a low LOD of 0.33 µM (Fig. 6 ).

Table S3 presents the linear range, LODs, and other performance metrics of various fluoroprobes used for TC detection. The as-constructed Met-CQDs demonstrates remarkable sensitivity, a well-defined linear correlation, rapid and portable sensing, and thus indicates a promising prospect for successful TC detection in food.

The TC-induced quenching mechanism was thoroughly elucidated using a combination of UV–vis spectra, fluorescence lifetime measurement, the Stern–Volmer equation and other analytical techniques. The UV–vis spectra of TC (380 nm) strongly coincided with the excitation spectra of the Met-CQDs (360 nm) (Fig. 7 a), suggesting a possible interaction between Met-CQDs and TC molecule via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE. Furthermore, comparison of the UV–vis spectra of the Met-CQDs in the absence of quenchers (without TC) and with TC revealed the appearance of two new peaks upon addition of TC (Fig. 7b), indicating a potential static quenching process. In the presence/absence of TC, the fluorescence-decay curves of Met-CQDs were analyzed in detail to distinguish between dynamic and static quenching, as well as IFE and FRET mechanisms. The fluorescence-decay curves of Met-CQDs overlapped in the presence/absence of TC (Fig. 7d). The average fluorescence lifetime (τ) was determined by fitting the decay curves using a double exponential model, as detailed in TEXT 4 in the SI. The τ values for Met-CQDs were estimated to be 1.09 ns at excitation wavelength of 420 nm in the absence of TC and 0.71 ns in the presence of TC, with no considerable variations. This consistency of fluorescence lifetime after and before the addition of TC supports the concept of static quenching.

To better elucidate the quenching process, the relationship between the FIs of Met-CQDs and the concentrations of TC (the quencher) was established according to the Stern–Volmer equation. The plot of F0/F1 vs. TC concentrations (0.1–500 μM) was obtained by the Stern–Volmer equation expressed as:
（1）
F 0
/
F 1
=
K
sv
C + 1

where Ksv and C represent the quenching constant and TC concentration (µmol L−1), respectively. As shown in Fig. 7c, the slope of the fitted linear regression was computed to be 0.003, corresponding to the Ksv value. Utilizing the bimolecular quenching equation (KSV = kqτ 0), the resulting quenching rate (kq , L mol−1 s−1) value was estimated to be 3.0 × 1012, which is significantly higher than 2 × 1010 [42]. Furthermore, the Ksv values decreased with increasing temperatures (Fig. S7), implying occurrence of the static-quenching phenomenon.

Besides, we performed a comparative FT-IR analysis on Met-CQDs and their mixture with TC. No noticeable changes or new peaks were observed (Fig. 2d), suggesting the production of a non-luminescent compound owing to the binding of Met-CQDs with TC molecules via static quenching. Meanwhile, the negative zeta potential recorded for the Met-CQDs could be related to surface carboxyl groups (Fig. 7e). After the addition of TC, this zeta potential increased, indicating that the Met-CQDs were well dispersed in the TC solution. In conclusion, the quenching mechanisms for Met-CQDs are derived from IFE and static quenching.

The as-constructed fluoroprobe was utilized for the detection of TC residues in various food products (e.g. milk, honey, and tap water) by means of either a fluorometer or a smartphone. The pretreatment and detection procedures were carried out as described in Section 2.6. However, the TC residues were not found in the three real samples. Subsequently, TC was spiked at three levels (10, 20, and 50 µmol L−1), following the assay steps as described in Section 2.5. The fluorometric method provided satisfactory recoveries for TC in food products, which ranged from 96.3 % to 101.4 % with RSDs < 2.89 % (Table 1 ), indicating a high precision and reproducibility of the Met-CQDs fluoroprobe.

To verify the detection accuracy of TC using the fluorometric approach, conventional liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) was utilized to measure TC in both real and spiked samples. The detailed conditions for the LC-MS/MS detection of TC are listed in TEXT5 and Table S2 in the SI. As the LC-MS/MS technique showed a low LOD for TC, the obtained solution was diluted 100-fold to ensure that TC concentration remained in a detectable range. Chromatograms illustrating TC in both real and spiked samples are depicted in Fig. S8. The residual concentration of TC in all three actual samples analyzed by LC-MS/MS varied between 0.002 and 0.009 μM. Moreover, a comparison of the TC levels detected by the newly developed method with those by LC-MS/MS revealed a commendable relative accuracy (83.03 %–103.49 %) for TC assays using the fluorometric method, and an accuracy of 85.85 %–102.51 % for TC with RGB method (Table 2 ). Both the RGB method and the fluorometric analysis exhibited a significantly positive correlation with LC-MS/MS for the quantification of TC in spiked samples (R2 > 0.99, p < 0.05, Fig. S9), indicating that this approach can achieve accuracy comparable to the LC-MS/MS. In summary, the Met-CQDs-based fluoroprobe has great promising in routine detection or monitoring of TCs.

A simple and efficient strategy that utilized methionine as the dopant was adopted to synthesize Met-CQDs with a high fluorescence quantum yield. The blue-emitting light from the Met-CQDs rapidly decreased upon the addition of TCs. This property enabled the design of a Met-CQDs-based fluoroprobe for TC detection via both a fluorometer and a smartphone. In the “ON-OFF” sensing model, the Met-CQDs-based fluoroprobe displayed specific selectivity, high precision, real-time portability, and rapid response (only 1 min). The quenching of the Met-CQDs was involved in both IFE and static quenching. Moreover, there is a significant positive correlation between the newly developed method and LC-MS/MS method in analyzing TC in spiked samples. This correlation indicates the effectiveness of the proposed approach and highlights its potential to provide a reliable framework for the portable monitoring of antibiotics residues in food products.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research received collaborative support from several entities, including the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22076134 and 32202148), the Jiangsu Provincial Key Research & Development Program (BE2022733), the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20211338), as well as the Key Science and Technology Project of Suzhou City (SS202028). Additionally, support came from Suzhou National Joint Laboratory focused on Green and Low-carbon Wastewater Treatment and Resource Utilization Technology (SZLSDT202404).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125498.
